task:
AUDIT ONLY — read-only investigation, do NOT change behavior in this atom. Goal: produce the authoritative map of daemon global state that assumes a single active run, which later atoms will harden. 

WHAT TO DO:
1. Trace the daemon run-driver, run-lifecycle, and reload paths. Find ctx.inFlight (the Map<workspaceId, runId>) and enumerate EVERY read of it: every `inFlight.has(...)`, `inFlight.get(...)`, `inFlight.size`, `inFlight.set/delete`. For each call site give file:line and one line on what it guards.
2. Classify each inFlight-coupled check as either (a) LEGITIMATELY GLOBAL — must wait on any run in flight regardless of workspace (e.g. engine/daemon self-reload), or (b) MUST-BE-PER-WORKSPACE — currently uses a global signal (e.g. `inFlight.size > 0`) but should be keyed by workspaceId so a run on workspace A cannot block workspace B. Justify each classification in one line.
3. Enumerate other mutable singletons in the daemon that assume one active run. Known suspects to confirm or clear with file:line evidence: `daemonReload` (single pending slot), the UI-bundle rebuild, any 'current run' assumption in the dashboard/status feed. For each: is it per-run/per-workspace safe, or a cross-run hazard?
4. Confirm (with file:line) whether stopControllers, the WAL store, the atomic run-counter, and cmux surface tracking are already per-run/per-workspace safe, as the priority claims. If any is NOT, flag it.

DELIVERABLE: write the audit as markdown to `cocoder/runs/146-run_287/audit-global-state.md` with a table per section (file:line | what it does | classification | reasoning). End with a prioritized list of the concrete hardening atoms this audit implies, smallest-blast-radius first.

ACCEPTANCE: every claim is backed by a real file:line that I can open and confirm; the inFlight call-site enumeration is COMPLETE (cross-check with a grep, don't trust any prior list); each site is classified global-vs-per-workspace with reasoning. MUST NOT: change any runtime behavior, edit any file outside `cocoder/runs/146-run_287/`. Report the grep command(s) you used so I can re-run them.
